content.full_text:
State of California

Memorandum

To : File                                          Date : Jan. 3, 1979


From : FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION           M-79-115
        Sarah Cameron

Subject: Nexus

        We were asked whether a member of the State Bar Board of
Governors should participate in the Board's decision whether to
take a position on a piece of legislation when he is the executive
director of an association of attorneys which has already taken
a position on the legislation.  Dan, Ted and I concluded that
if part of his duties as executive director is to lobby for
the association's positions on legislation, i.e., to carry out
the association's policies, then any actions he would take to
gain support of the State Bar for the legislation would be
furthering the policies of the association's policies as well.
There is therefore a nexus between his actions on the State Bar
and his duties as executive director of the association.

        Another example mentioned in our conversation was a vari-
ation of one of the fact situations presented in the Thorner
opinion.  In that opinion, it was decided that it was not reason-
ably foreseeable that the decision would have an effect on a
construction company which was a source of income to the official.
The official's husband was a project engineer for the construction
company and was working on a project which could not be affected
by the decision under consideration, which was whether to lift
the moritorium on new sewer connections.  If, however, he were
an attorney for the company and part of his duties were to lobby
local agencies against such moritoriums, then there would be a
nexus and the official would have to disqualify herself.

        Another example, in San Jose, one of the city councilmen is
putting together a deal between a property owner and a developer
with respect to some properties near a redevelopment area of the
city.  For his services he will receive a finder's fee.  If the
development is of the kind which was not going on in the area
before redevelopment began, there is a nexus between his decisions
promoting and implementing redevelopment and his income for bringing
a developer and a property owner together.

STC:nrw

Note: He also by question, Dave later informed that
      person in question ask he readily duties but only admitting
      trustee role.  S-Trustee agreed that there was no nexus.

sections.question_synthetic: Whether public officials have a conflict of interest (nexus) when their duties in other positions could be furthered by their official actions, using three hypothetical scenarios involving a State Bar Board member, construction company employment, and municipal redevelopment.
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The FPPC concluded that a nexus exists when an official's actions on a public board would further policies or economic interests tied to their other employment, requiring disqualification; however, purely administrative duties without lobbying responsibilities do not create such a nexus.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Internal memorandum rather than formal advice letter response; no formal QUESTION/CONCLUSION sections; contains three hypothetical examples and a corrective note about the State Bar scenario; addressee is 'File' indicating internal communication.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: Whether public officials have a conflict of interest (nexus) when their duties in other positions could be furthered by their official actions, using three hypothetical scenarios involving a State Bar Board member, construction company employment, and municipal redevelopment.

CONCLUSION: The FPPC concluded that a nexus exists when an official's actions on a public board would further policies or economic interests tied to their other employment, requiring disqualification; however, purely administrative duties without lobbying responsibilities do not create such a nexus.
embedding.first_500_words: **Memorandum** **State of California** **To:** File **Date:** Jan. 3, 1979 **From:** FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION Sarah Cameron **Subject:** Nexus We were asked whether a member of the State Bar Board of Governors should participate in the Board's decision whether to take a position on a piece of legislation when he is the executive director of an association of attorneys which has already taken a position on the legislation. Dan, Ted and I concluded that if part of his duties as executive director is to lobby for the association's positions on legislation, i.e., to carry out the association's policies, then any actions he would take to gain support of the State Bar for the legislation would be furthering the policies of the association's policies as well. There is therefore a nexus between his actions on the State Bar and his duties as executive director of the association. Another example mentioned in our conversation was a variation of one of the fact situations presented in the Thorner opinion. In that opinion, it was decided that it was not reasonably foreseeable that the decision would have an effect on a construction company which was a source of income to the official. The official's husband was a project engineer for the construction company and was working on a project which could not be affected by the decision under consideration, which was whether to lift the moratorium on new sewer connections. If, however, he were an attorney for the company and part of his duties were to lobby local agencies against such moratoriums, then there would be a nexus and the official would have to disqualify herself. Another example, in San Jose, one of the city councilmen is putting together a deal between a property owner and a developer with respect to some properties near a redevelopment area of the city. For his services he will receive a finder's fee. If the development is of the kind which was not going on in the area before redevelopment began, there is a nexus between his decisions promoting and implementing redevelopment and his income for bringing a developer and a property owner together. STC:nrw Note: re State Bar question, I was later informed that person in question had no lobbying duties but only administrative ones. I therefore agreed that there was no nexus.
embedding.summary: This internal FPPC memorandum addresses the concept of 'nexus' in conflict of interest analysis, concluding that public officials must disqualify themselves when their official actions would further the interests of their outside employment, with a later note clarifying that administrative-only duties lack the necessary nexus.
